system_prompt:
You are Sunstein, a precise and efficient AI assistant.

Rules:
- Be concise. Skip explanations unless asked.
- Minimize token usage in every response.
- Use execute_code for batch operations, not one-at-a-time.
- Always use the cheapest model that can handle the task.
- Compress context regularly with /compress.
- Never repeat information already in the conversation.
- One-word answers when possible.
- No preamble, no postamble.

If the user asks about configuring, setting up, or using Hermes Agent itself, load the `hermes-agent` skill with skill_view(name='hermes-agent') before answering. Docs: https://hermes-agent.nousresearch.com/docs

You have persistent memory across sessions. Save durable facts using the memory tool: user preferences, environment details, tool quirks, and stable conventions. Memory is injected into every turn, so keep it compact and focused on facts that will still matter later.
Prioritize what reduces future user steering — the most valuable memory is one that prevents the user from having to correct or remind you again. User preferences and recurring corrections matter more than procedural task details.
Do NOT save task progress, session outcomes, completed-work logs, or temporary TODO state to memory; use session_search to recall those from past transcripts. If you've discovered a new way to do something, solved a problem that could be necessary later, save it as a skill with the skill tool.
Write memories as declarative facts, not instructions to yourself. 'User prefers concise responses' ✓ — 'Always respond concisely' ✗. 'Project uses pytest with xdist' ✓ — 'Run tests with pytest -n 4' ✗. Imperative phrasing gets re-read as a directive in later sessions and can cause repeated work or override the user's current request. Procedures and workflows belong in skills, not memory. After completing a complex task (5+ tool calls), fixing a tricky error, or discovering a non-trivial workflow, save the approach as a skill with skill_manage so you can reuse it next time.
When using a skill and finding it outdated, incomplete, or wrong, patch it immediately with skill_manage(action='patch') — don't wait to be asked. Skills that aren't maintained become liabilities.

function.name: memory
function.description:
Save durable information to persistent memory that survives across sessions. Memory is injected into future turns, so keep it compact and focused on facts that will still matter later.

WHEN TO SAVE (do this proactively, don't wait to be asked):
- User corrects you or says 'remember this' / 'don't do that again'
- User shares a preference, habit, or personal detail (name, role, timezone, coding style)
- You discover something about the environment (OS, installed tools, project structure)
- You learn a convention, API quirk, or workflow specific to this user's setup
- You identify a stable fact that will be useful again in future sessions

PRIORITY: User preferences and corrections > environment facts > procedural knowledge. The most valuable memory prevents the user from having to repeat themselves.

Do NOT save task progress, session outcomes, completed-work logs, or temporary TODO state to memory; use session_search to recall those from past transcripts.
If you've discovered a new way to do something, solved a problem that could be necessary later, save it as a skill with the skill tool.

TWO TARGETS:
- 'user': who the user is -- name, role, preferences, communication style, pet peeves
- 'memory': your notes -- environment facts, project conventions, tool quirks, lessons learned

ACTIONS: add (new entry), replace (update existing -- old_text identifies it), remove (delete -- old_text identifies it).

SKIP: trivial/obvious info, things easily re-discovered, raw data dumps, and temporary task state.

target.description: Which memory store: 'memory' for personal notes, 'user' for user profile.

content.description: The entry content. Required for 'add' and 'replace'.

function.name: skill_manage
function.description:
Manage skills (create, update, delete). Skills are your procedural memory — reusable approaches for recurring task types. New skills go to ~/.hermes/skills/; existing skills can be modified wherever they live.

Actions: create (full SKILL.md + optional category), patch (old_string/new_string — preferred for fixes), edit (full SKILL.md rewrite — major overhauls only), delete, write_file, remove_file.

Create when: complex task succeeded (5+ calls), errors overcome, user-corrected approach worked, non-trivial workflow discovered, or user asks you to remember a procedure.
Update when: instructions stale/wrong, OS-specific failures, missing steps or pitfalls found during use. If you used a skill and hit issues not covered by it, patch it immediately.

After difficult/iterative tasks, offer to save as a skill. Skip for simple one-offs. Confirm with user before creating/deleting.

Good skills: trigger conditions, numbered steps with exact commands, pitfalls section, verification steps. Use skill_view() to see format examples.

Pinned skills are off-limits — all write actions refuse with a message pointing the user to `hermes curator unpin <name>`. Don't try to route around this by renaming or recreating.

name.description: Skill name (lowercase, hyphens/underscores, max 64 chars). Must match an existing skill for patch/edit/delete/write_file/remove_file.

content.description: Full SKILL.md content (YAML frontmatter + markdown body). Required for 'create' and 'edit'. For 'edit', read the skill first with skill_view() and provide the complete updated text.

old_string.description: Text to find in the file (required for 'patch'). Must be unique unless replace_all=true. Include enough surrounding context to ensure uniqueness.

new_string.description: Replacement text (required for 'patch'). Can be empty string to delete the matched text.

replace_all.description: For 'patch': replace all occurrences instead of requiring a unique match (default: false).

category.description: Optional category/domain for organizing the skill (e.g., 'devops', 'data-science', 'mlops'). Creates a subdirectory grouping. Only used with 'create'.

file_path.description: Path to a supporting file within the skill directory. For 'write_file'/'remove_file': required, must be under references/, templates/, scripts/, or assets/. For 'patch': optional, defaults to SKILL.md if omitted.

file_content.description: Content for the file. Required for 'write_file'.

function.name: skill_view
function.description: Skills allow for loading information about specific tasks and workflows, as well as scripts and templates. Load a skill's full content or access its linked files (references, templates, scripts). First call returns SKILL.md content plus a 'linked_files' dict showing available references/templates/scripts. To access those, call again with file_path parameter.

name.description: The skill name (use skills_list to see available skills). For plugin-provided skills, use the qualified form 'plugin:skill' (e.g. 'superpowers:writing-plans').

file_path.description: OPTIONAL: Path to a linked file within the skill (e.g., 'references/api.md', 'templates/config.yaml', 'scripts/validate.py'). Omit to get the main SKILL.md content.

function.name: skills_list
function.description: List available skills (name + description). Use skill_view(name) to load full content.

category.description: Optional category filter to narrow results

# Client Site Improvement Roadmap

Audit a live client website (URL + repo) systematically — inspect every page, dive into the codebase, cross-reference against any existing research/plans/ROAST docs, and produce a prioritized improvement list across tiers.

## When to Use

- Client site is live but you've never reviewed it end-to-end
- Client asked for "upgrade ideas" or "what's missing"
- Taking over maintenance of an existing client site
- Between build phases — assess current state before deciding next steps
- After a ROAST or UX critique was done previously — verify fixes and find new gaps

## Process

### Phase 0: Gather All Existing Assets

Before touching the site, inventory everything:

1. **Repo** — check both org accounts (Ai-Whisperers, IvanWeissVanDerPol)
2. **Research docs** — `/root/research/web-plans/<client>-*` for competitive analysis and build plans
3. **Content folders** — `/root/<client>-content/` for pre-written SEO content
4. **ROAST.md** — if exists in repo root, read it. It contains the last audit's findings
5. **AGENTS.md** — dev guidelines, brand identity, coding standards
6. **README.md** — project overview and tech stack

### Phase 1: Live Site Inspection

Browse every major page of the live site:

**Homepage:**
- Hero headline + CTA (WhatsApp? Phone? Both?)
- Trust signals visible above fold (years, rating, hospital)
- Service grid — how many services? Are they real or generic?
- Live testimonials or placeholder text?
- Cookie consent present?
- Floating WhatsApp button?

**Service pages** (`/servicios`, `/servicios/[slug]`):
- Breadcrumb navigation?
- Individual SEO metadata (check <title> in browser)
- "Consultar por WhatsApp" with service name pre-filled?

**About / Team** (`/sobre-nosotros`):
- Real doctor photos or emoji/placeholder icons?
- Credentials present? Years of experience?
- Second team member mentioned?

**Pricing** (`/precios`):
- Reference prices in local currency?
- Payment methods listed?
- Financing options?

**Gallery** (`/galeria`):
- Real before/after images or placeholder text ("Antes: dientes faltantes")?
- Filter by treatment type?
- Patient consent notice?

**Booking / Appointment** (`/agendar-cita`):
- Multi-step flow? What steps?
- Does it save to database or just UI?

**Contact** (`/contacto`):
- Working contact form? What fields?
- Google Maps embed with correct address?
- WhatsApp + phone + email displayed?

**Blog** (`/blog`):
- Real or AI-generated posts?
- Categories? Authors? Dates?
- CTA at end of posts?

**Dental tourism page** (`/turismo-dental`):
- Price comparison table (USD)?
- English language option?

**Other specialty pages** — emergency, FAQ, first visit, patient forms, testimonials, dental assessment quiz

**Footer:**
- Social links (empty strings = no real accounts)
- Privacy/terms links
- Hours, address, NAP consistency with constants.ts
- Copyright

**Edge cases:**
- Subdomain routing (duerksen.paragu-ai.com vs clinicaduerksen.com.py)
- English version if available (`/en`)
- Landing pages (`/lp/`)

### Phase 2: Codebase Inspection

After browsing the live site, check the code for what's different:

**Constants** (`lib/constants.ts`):
- Social links — empty strings vs filled
- Phone/WhatsApp — single source of truth?
- **phoneRaw trap** — some codebases have a `phoneRaw` field separate from `phone`. Check if `phoneRaw` is a real number or an obscured placeholder (e.g. `+595****8755`). `tel:` links should use the real diallable number, not an obscured version.
- Address/geo — correct coordinates?

**API routes** (`app/api/`):

**Supabase usage:** `grep -r 'supabase\|createClient\|from(' app/ --include='*.tsx' -l`
- If 0 results, the data layer is NOT wired up (everything hardcoded)

**shadcn/ui:** `ls components/ui/`
- If empty despite `components.json` existing, UI components aren't installed

**JSON-LD/structured data:** `grep -r 'json-ld\|JsonLd' app/layout.tsx`
- If the component exists in `components/seo/` but isn't imported, Google gets no rich results

**Error boundaries:** `ls app/error.tsx app/not-found.tsx app/loading.tsx`
- Missing error.tsx = full white screen on crash
- Missing not-found.tsx = default Next.js 404
- Missing loading.tsx = no loading skeleton

**Framer Motion:** `grep 'framer-motion' app/ --include='*.tsx' -rl`
- If present in many components, ~45KB of unnecessary JS (CSS animations are free)

**Social links in constants:** Check if values are empty strings

**Page inventory:**
```bash
find app -name 'page.tsx' | sed 's|app/||;s|/page.tsx||' | sort
```
Cross-reference with nav links in the live site. Nav may link to ghost pages.

### Phase 3: Cross-Reference Against Research

Compare what's live against the research/plans:

**From competitive analysis (100-site benchmark):**
- Feature priority matrix: WhatsApp (mandatory), Google Maps (90%), Reviews widget (86%), Team profiles (80%)
- Are any of these missing or broken?

**From build plan:**
- Phases completed vs planned (MVP → V1 → V2)
- What phases were planned but never executed?

**From ROAST.md:**
- Which previous issues are STILL unresolved?
- Which were fixed?

### Phase 4: Structure the Roadmap

Organize improvements into 5 tiers:

**TIER 1: FIX THE HOLLOW SHELL** ($0, must fix immediately)
- Real photos replacing placeholder/emoji
- Real content replacing AI-generated filler
- Wire up the data layer (Supabase)
- Import/activate dead components (JSON-LD, shadcn/ui)
- Add error boundaries + loading states
- Remove performance-killing JS bloat (framer-motion)
- Fix social links (empty strings → real URLs)

**TIER 2: CONTENT & CREDIBILITY** (Low cost, high trust impact)
- Google Business Profile (claim + verify — often unclaimed)
- Google Reviews widget (replace fake testimonials with real ones)
- Patient intake form (digital version of paper forms)
- Insurance/payment details with plan logos
- New patient special / first visit offer
- Real before/after slider
- FAQ with schema markup
- Video testimonials (even phone-shot)

**TIER 3: CONVERSION & LEAD GENERATION** (Medium effort, patient impact)
- WhatsApp workflow automation (auto-reply with options)
- Appointment booking wired to Supabase + WhatsApp notification
- Abandoned booking recovery
- Assessment quiz → personalized recommendation → booking
- Treatment comparison tool
- Emergency page with clear after-hours protocol
- Dental tourism booking funnel
- Exit-intent popup with offer

**TIER 4: TECHNICAL & UX** (Developer effort, quality impact)
- Install and use shadcn/ui components
- Add loading skeletons (loading.tsx per page)
- Accessibility audit (skip-to-content, aria, focus trap, reduced-motion)
- Mobile menu focus management
- Performance (Lighthouse audit, image optimization, bundle analysis)
- TypeScript strictness audit
- Test suite (smoke tests for critical routes)
- CI pipeline (GitHub Actions: build + TS check + ESLint + Lighthouse)
- Docker network config verification
- Sitemap + robots.txt verification

**TIER 5: GROWTH & RETENTION** (Business impact, revenue)
- Review generation system (post-treatment WhatsApp ask)
- Appointment reminders (24h before)
- Post-treatment follow-up (48h check-in)
- 6-month recall system
- Birthday messages with discount
- Newsletter signup
- Referral program
- Staff/team page with photos
- A/B testing on hero + CTA
- Heatmap + session recording (Microsoft Clarity)

### Phase 5: Priority Ranking

Assign each tier a timeframe and note dependencies:

```
CRITICAL (this week)    : items from Tiers 1 + 2 that block everything else
HIGH (this month)       : remaining Tiers 1 + 2 + highest Tier 3
MEDIUM (next month)     : rest of Tier 3 + most of Tier 4
GROWTH (ongoing)        : Tier 5 (never really "done")
```

## Output Format

Write the roadmap as a structured markdown document. Each item should have:
- Numbered ID
- Brief description of the gap
- The evidence (what's broken/missing)
- Quick win indicator (can be done without client input)

## Parallel Implementation While Waiting for Client Data

When the improvement roadmap reveals the #1 blocker is client data (photos, prices, social links), you can still implement everything that doesn't need that data. Split work:

### Do NOW (no client data needed)

| Category | What to implement |
|----------|------------------|
| **Error handling** | not-found.tsx, loading.tsx for all page groups |
| **SEO foundation** | JSON-LD (fix phone, add aggregateRating, sameAs, hasMap), sitemap.ts, robots.ts |
| **Form infrastructure** | Zod validation schemas, rate limiting, honeypot, server actions |
| **Accessibility** | focus trap on mobile menu, Escape key, aria-modal, body scroll lock, skip-to-content, prefers-reduced-motion |
| **CI/CD** | GitHub Actions workflow (typecheck + lint + build) |
| **Docker** | Fix host rules, add healthcheck endpoint |
| **Performance** | Remove framer-motion, CSS animations, bundle analysis |
| **TypeScript hardening** | Audit for `any` types, fix non-null assertions |

### Do AFTER client delivers data (blocked on questionnaire)

| Category | What they need to send |
|----------|----------------------|
| **Photos** | Team headshots, location, before/after cases, consent forms |
| **Content** | Verify/correct bio, team member profiles, staff list |
| **Pricing** | Verify price ranges, payment methods, insurance plans |
| **Social** | Facebook/Instagram URLs, GBP claimed status |
| **Operations** | WhatsApp workflow, preferred contact method, CMS login |

### Expected ratio

Typically 40-50% of the work can be done independently. The client\'s 1-2 hours of questionnaire time is the single gate.

### Output two docs

After the audit + roadmap, create two separate files in `docs/`:

1. **`docs/cliente-cuestionario.md`** — The questionnaire in the client's language. Ready to send as-is. Include: how to submit (WhatsApp, Google Drive), photo quality guidelines, estimated completion time.

2. **`docs/plan-implementacion-completo.md`** — The full 5-phase plan with:
   - All tiers consolidated into numbered phases
   - Effort estimates per phase (hours)
   - Dependency graph (which phases block which)
   - Phase 0 (client data collection) explicitly marked as the single gate
   - Deployment checklist at the end
   - Cost breakdown
   - Table format for each phase with: task ID, description, file/component, estimated time

### When GitHub push fails

The repo may be archived on GitHub. In that case:
1. Commit locally regardless: `git add -A && git commit -m "..."`  
2. Document the commit in memory so future sessions know the local state
3. Deploy via Docker anyway — the build + service update works from the local repo
4. The code is live even if GitHub doesn't have the latest

## Pitfalls

- **Repo may be archived/read-only on GitHub** — before pushing, try `git push origin main`. If you get a 403 "archived so it's read-only", the commit still lives locally. Resolution: create a new repo with `gh repo create {org}/{name} --private --push --source=.`, or ask the org owner via Telegram/WhatsApp to un-archive. If neither works, keep the commit local and note it in memory — do NOT delete the local repo.
- **All content may be placeholder/AI-generated** — verify by checking for emoji in doctor photos, generic testimonials, "Antes:" labels without images, empty social links

## Reference Files

- `references/client-data-questionnaire-template.md` — reusable 6-section questionnaire to send clients when their site is content-empty (missing photos, fake testimonials, AI-generated text). Adapt the sections to the client's industry before sending.
- Real example: `/root/clinica-duerksen/docs/cliente-cuestionario.md` — a completed questionnaire for a dental clinic with before/after photo consent forms, IPS/insurance pricing, and dental tourism sections. Use as reference when adapting the template for similar health/service businesses.

## Pitfalls
- **Supabase client may exist but nothing uses it** — check `app/` for actual queries, not just lib/ for client initialization
- **JSON-LD component may exist but never be imported** — check app/layout.tsx specifically
- **WhatsApp number duplication** — check if it exists in both constants.ts and .env.example, and whether any component hardcodes it
- **Network mismatch on deploy** — docker-compose.yml may reference a network that doesn't exist on the VPS (`traefik-public` vs `agent-net`)
- **Nav links may 404** — header/footer often have links to pages that don't exist yet
- **`phoneRaw` trap** — some codebases have a `phoneRaw` field in constants.ts separate from `phone`. This field is meant for `tel:` links (URL-safe, no spaces, no special chars). But it may be set to an obscured placeholder like `+595****8755` instead of the real number. Check it: `grep 'phoneRaw' lib/constants.ts`. If obscured, fix it: `phoneRaw: BUSinesS.phone.replace(/[^+\\d]/g, '')` or hardcode the dialable number. Without this fix, `tel:` links on the site dial a wrong number.

    10|## A. Identidad y Posicionamiento
    11|
    12|### A.1 ¿Cuál es el nombre legal de su firma? ¿Y el nombre comercial (si es diferente)?

    20|### A.2 ¿Cómo describiría su servicio en una frase para un inversor extranjero?

    67|- B) 4-10
    68|- C) 11-25
    69|- D) 26-50
    70|- E) 50+
    71|
    72|### B.3 ¿Tienen algún caso de éxito o proyecto emblemático que podamos destacar?
    73|
    74|- A) Sí, tenemos 1-2 casos concretos que podemos compartir
    75|- B) Sí, tenemos múltiples casos documentados
    76|- C) Tenemos referencias pero no casos públicos
    77|- D) Todavía no tenemos casos para compartir
    78|- E) Preferimos mantener la confidencialidad
    79|
    80|---
    81|
    82|## C. Audiencia y Contenido

    85|
    86|- A) High-net-worth individuals (HNWI, $1M+) buscando segunda residencia

    91|

{"path": "/root/golden-visa-advisory/docs/golden-visa-improvement-roadmap.md", "content": "# Golden Visa Advisory — Improvement Roadmap

**Status:** Live at goldenvisa.paragu-ai.com  
**Client:** Raúl Fretes (WA group [REDACTED_PHONE])  
**Audit Date:** [REDACTED_PHONE]  
**Stack:** Next.js 16.2, React 19, Tailwind CSS 4, TypeScript, Docker Swarm

---

## Current Score: 5/10

The site is functional but feels like a generic SaaS landing page template. Missing: visual identity, emotional design, real photos, interactivity, and conversion infrastructure. It looks like a placeholder — not an authoritative advisory firm.

---

## TIER 0: CRITICAL BUGS & BLOCKERS (Do today)

| # | Issue | Evidence | Fix |
|---|-------|----------|-----|
| 0.1 | WhatsApp number is placeholder `[REDACTED_PHONE]` | Hardcoded in BusinessLanding.tsx:8 and InvestorLanding.tsx:9 — commented as "placeholder" | Get real number from Raúl |
| 0.2 | No error.tsx or not-found.tsx | Site has `_global-error` page built by Next.js but no custom 404 | Add `src/app/not-found.tsx` |
| 0.3 | No loading.tsx | No loading skeletons anywhere | Add `src/app/loading.tsx` |
| 0.4 | No sitemap.xml or robots.txt | SEO bots can't discover the site | Add `src/app/sitemap.ts` + `src/app/robots.ts` |
| 0.5 | No JSON-LD structured data | Google Knowledge Panel won't show for the firm | Add Organization + LocalBusiness schema in layout.tsx |

# Client Site Improvement Roadmap

Audit a live client website (URL + repo) systematically — inspect every page, dive into the codebase, cross-reference against any existing research/plans/ROAST docs, and produce a prioritized improvement list across tiers.

## When to Use

- Client site is live but you've never reviewed it end-to-end
- Client asked for "upgrade ideas" or "what's missing"
- Taking over maintenance of an existing client site
- Between build phases — assess current state before deciding next steps
- After a ROAST or UX critique was done previously — verify fixes and find new gaps

## Process

### Phase 0: Gather All Existing Assets

Before touching the site, inventory everything:

1. **Repo** — check both org accounts (Ai-Whisperers, IvanWeissVanDerPol)
2. **Research docs** — `/root/research/web-plans/<client>-*` for competitive analysis and build plans
3. **Content folders** — `/root/<client>-content/` for pre-written SEO content
4. **ROAST.md** — if exists in repo root, read it. It contains the last audit's findings
5. **AGENTS.md** — dev guidelines, brand identity, coding standards
6. **README.md** — project overview and tech stack

### Phase 1: Live Site Inspection

Browse every major page of the live site:

**Homepage:**
- Hero headline + CTA (WhatsApp? Phone? Both?)
- Trust signals visible above fold (years, rating, hospital)
- Service grid — how many services? Are they real or generic?
- Live testimonials or placeholder text?
- Cookie consent present?
- Floating WhatsApp button?

**Service pages** (`/servicios`, `/servicios/[slug]`):
- Breadcrumb navigation?
- Individual SEO metadata (check <title> in browser)
- "Consultar por WhatsApp" with service name pre-filled?

**About / Team** (`/sobre-nosotros`):
- Real doctor photos or emoji/placeholder icons?
- Credentials present? Years of experience?
- Second team member mentioned?

**Pricing** (`/precios`):
- Reference prices in local currency?
- Payment methods listed?
- Financing options?

**Gallery** (`/galeria`):
- Real before/after images or placeholder text ("Antes: dientes faltantes")?
- Filter by treatment type?
- Patient consent notice?

**Booking / Appointment** (`/agendar-cita`):
- Multi-step flow? What steps?
- Does it save to database or just UI?

**Contact** (`/contacto`):
- Working contact form? What fields?
- Google Maps embed with correct address?
- WhatsApp + phone + email displayed?

**Blog** (`/blog`):
- Real or AI-generated posts?
- Categories? Authors? Dates?
- CTA at end of posts?

**Dental tourism page** (`/turismo-dental`):
- Price comparison table (USD)?
- English language option?

**Other specialty pages** — emergency, FAQ, first visit, patient forms, testimonials, dental assessment quiz

**Footer:**
- Social links (empty strings = no real accounts)
- Privacy/terms links
- Hours, address, NAP consistency with constants.ts
- Copyright

**Edge cases:**
- Subdomain routing (duerksen.paragu-ai.com vs clinicaduerksen.com.py)
- English version if available (`/en`)
- Landing pages (`/lp/`)

### Phase 2: Codebase Inspection

After browsing the live site, check the code for what's different:

**Constants** (`lib/constants.ts`):
- Social links — empty strings vs filled
- Phone/WhatsApp — single source of truth?
- **phoneRaw trap** — some codebases have a `phoneRaw` field separate from `phone`. Check if `phoneRaw` is a real number or an obscured placeholder (e.g. `+595****8755`). `tel:` links should use the real diallable number, not an obscured version.
- Address/geo — correct coordinates?

**API routes** (`app/api/`):

**Supabase usage:** `grep -r 'supabase\|createClient\|from(' app/ --include='*.tsx' -l`
- If 0 results, the data layer is NOT wired up (everything hardcoded)

**shadcn/ui:** `ls components/ui/`
- If empty despite `components.json` existing, UI components aren't installed

**JSON-LD/structured data:** `grep -r 'json-ld\|JsonLd' app/layout.tsx`
- If the component exists in `components/seo/` but isn't imported, Google gets no rich results

**Error boundaries:** `ls app/error.tsx app/not-found.tsx app/loading.tsx`
- Missing error.tsx = full white screen on crash
- Missing not-found.tsx = default Next.js 404
- Missing loading.tsx = no loading skeleton

**Framer Motion:** `grep 'framer-motion' app/ --include='*.tsx' -rl`
- If present in many components, ~45KB of unnecessary JS (CSS animations are free)

**Social links in constants:** Check if values are empty strings

**Page inventory:**
```bash
find app -name 'page.tsx' | sed 's|app/||;s|/page.tsx||' | sort
```
Cross-reference with nav links in the live site. Nav may link to ghost pages.

### Phase 3: Cross-Reference Against Research

Compare what's live against the research/plans:

**From competitive analysis (100-site benchmark):**
- Feature priority matrix: WhatsApp (mandatory), Google Maps (90%), Reviews widget (86%), Team profiles (80%)
- Are any of these missing or broken?

**From build plan:**
- Phases completed vs planned (MVP → V1 → V2)
- What phases were planned but never executed?

**From ROAST.md:**
- Which previous issues are STILL unresolved?
- Which were fixed?

### Phase 4: Structure the Roadmap

Organize improvements into 5 tiers:

**TIER 1: FIX THE HOLLOW SHELL** ($0, must fix immediately)
- Real photos replacing placeholder/emoji
- Real content replacing AI-generated filler
- Wire up the data layer (Supabase)
- Import/activate dead components (JSON-LD, shadcn/ui)
- Add error boundaries + loading states
- Remove performance-killing JS bloat (framer-motion)
- Fix social links (empty strings → real URLs)

**TIER 2: CONTENT & CREDIBILITY** (Low cost, high trust impact)
- Google Business Profile (claim + verify — often unclaimed)
- Google Reviews widget (replace fake testimonials with real ones)
- Patient intake form (digital version of paper forms)
- Insurance/payment details with plan logos
- New patient special / first visit offer
- Real before/after slider
- FAQ with schema markup
- Video testimonials (even phone-shot)

**TIER 3: CONVERSION & LEAD GENERATION** (Medium effort, patient impact)
- WhatsApp workflow automation (auto-reply with options)
- Appointment booking wired to Supabase + WhatsApp notification
- Abandoned booking recovery
- Assessment quiz → personalized recommendation → booking
- Treatment comparison tool
- Emergency page with clear after-hours protocol
- Dental tourism booking funnel
- Exit-intent popup with offer

**TIER 4: TECHNICAL & UX** (Developer effort, quality impact)
- Install and use shadcn/ui components
- Add loading skeletons (loading.tsx per page)
- Accessibility audit (skip-to-content, aria, focus trap, reduced-motion)
- Mobile menu focus management
- Performance (Lighthouse audit, image optimization, bundle analysis)
- TypeScript strictness audit
- Test suite (smoke tests for critical routes)
- CI pipeline (GitHub Actions: build + TS check + ESLint + Lighthouse)
- Docker network config verification
- Sitemap + robots.txt verification

**TIER 5: GROWTH & RETENTION** (Business impact, revenue)
- Review generation system (post-treatment WhatsApp ask)
- Appointment reminders (24h before)
- Post-treatment follow-up (48h check-in)
- 6-month recall system
- Birthday messages with discount
- Newsletter signup
- Referral program
- Staff/team page with photos
- A/B testing on hero + CTA
- Heatmap + session recording (Microsoft Clarity)

### Phase 5: Priority Ranking

Assign each tier a timeframe and note dependencies:

```
CRITICAL (this week)    : items from Tiers 1 + 2 that block everything else
HIGH (this month)       : remaining Tiers 1 + 2 + highest Tier 3
MEDIUM (next month)     : rest of Tier 3 + most of Tier 4
GROWTH (ongoing)        : Tier 5 (never really "done")
```

## Output Format

Write the roadmap as a structured markdown document. Each item should have:
- Numbered ID
- Brief description of the gap
- The evidence (what's broken/missing)
- Quick win indicator (can be done without client input)

## Parallel Implementation While Waiting for Client Data

When the improvement roadmap reveals the #1 blocker is client data (photos, prices, social links), you can still implement everything that doesn't need that data. Split work:

### Do NOW (no client data needed)

| Category | What to implement |
|----------|------------------|
| **Error handling** | not-found.tsx, loading.tsx for all page groups |
| **SEO foundation** | JSON-LD (fix phone, add aggregateRating, sameAs, hasMap), sitemap.ts, robots.ts |
| **Form infrastructure** | Zod validation schemas, rate limiting, honeypot, server actions |
| **Accessibility** | focus trap on mobile menu, Escape key, aria-modal, body scroll lock, skip-to-content, prefers-reduced-motion |
| **CI/CD** | GitHub Actions workflow (typecheck + lint + build) |
| **Docker** | Fix host rules, add healthcheck endpoint |
| **Performance** | Remove framer-motion, CSS animations, bundle analysis |
| **TypeScript hardening** | Audit for `any` types, fix non-null assertions |

### Do AFTER client delivers data (blocked on questionnaire)

| Category | What they need to send |
|----------|----------------------|
| **Photos** | Team headshots, location, before/after cases, consent forms |
| **Content** | Verify/correct bio, team member profiles, staff list |
| **Pricing** | Verify price ranges, payment methods, insurance plans |
| **Social** | Facebook/Instagram URLs, GBP claimed status |
| **Operations** | WhatsApp workflow, preferred contact method, CMS login |

### Expected ratio

Typically 40-50% of the work can be done independently. The client\'s 1-2 hours of questionnaire time is the single gate.

### Output two docs

After the audit + roadmap, create two separate files in `docs/`:

1. **`docs/cliente-cuestionario.md`** — The questionnaire in the client's language. Ready to send as-is. Include: how to submit (WhatsApp, Google Drive), photo quality guidelines, estimated completion time.

2. **`docs/plan-implementacion-completo.md`** — The full 5-phase plan with:
   - All tiers consolidated into numbered phases
   - Effort estimates per phase (hours)
   - Dependency graph (which phases block which)
   - Phase 0 (client data collection) explicitly marked as the single gate
   - Deployment checklist at the end
   - Cost breakdown
   - Table format for each phase with: task ID, description, file/component, estimated time

### When GitHub push fails

The repo may be archived on GitHub. In that case:
1. Commit locally regardless: `git add -A && git commit -m "..."`  
2. Document the commit in memory so future sessions know the local state
3. Deploy via Docker anyway — the build + service update works from the local repo
4. The code is live even if GitHub doesn't have the latest

## Pitfalls

- **Repo may be archived/read-only on GitHub** — before pushing, try `git push origin main`. If you get a 403 "archived so it's read-only", the commit still lives locally. Resolution: create a new repo with `gh repo create {org}/{name} --private --push --source=.`, or ask the org owner via Telegram/WhatsApp to un-archive. If neither works, keep the commit local and note it in memory — do NOT delete the local repo.
- **All content may be placeholder/AI-generated** — verify by checking for emoji in doctor photos, generic testimonials, "Antes:" labels without images, empty social links

## Reference Files

- `references/client-data-questionnaire-template.md` — reusable 6-section questionnaire to send clients when their site is content-empty (missing photos, fake testimonials, AI-generated text). Adapt the sections to the client's industry before sending.
- Real example: `/root/clinica-duerksen/docs/cliente-cuestionario.md` — a completed questionnaire for a dental clinic with before/after photo consent forms, IPS/insurance pricing, and dental tourism sections. Use as reference when adapting the template for similar health/service businesses.

## Pitfalls
- **Supabase client may exist but nothing uses it** — check `app/` for actual queries, not just lib/ for client initialization
- **JSON-LD component may exist but never be imported** — check app/layout.tsx specifically
- **WhatsApp number duplication** — check if it exists in both constants.ts and .env.example, and whether any component hardcodes it
- **Network mismatch on deploy** — docker-compose.yml may reference a network that doesn't exist on the VPS (`traefik-public` vs `agent-net`)
- **Nav links may 404** — header/footer often have links to pages that don't exist yet
- **`phoneRaw` trap** — some codebases have a `phoneRaw` field in constants.ts separate from `phone`. This field is meant for `tel:` links (URL-safe, no spaces, no special chars). But it may be set to an obscured placeholder like `+595****8755` instead of the real number. Check it: `grep 'phoneRaw' lib/constants.ts`. If obscured, fix it: `phoneRaw: BUSinesS.phone.replace(/[^+\\d]/g, '')` or hardcode the dialable number. Without this fix, `tel:` links on the site dial a wrong number.
